body_txt: Science Fiction trope wherein ancient locations, legends, gods, and creatures from ancient myth are connected to alien visitors from a radically more advanced civilization. This trope includes that these aliens influenced our history in some way, mostly through technological advances . Often these aliens are sufficiently advanced . When this trope kicks in, expect various myths to be related to aliens . Everyone knows about Clarke's Third Law : the many legends of magic, gods, beings coming from the sky and the like were based on alien technology that we could not comprehend at that time. Proponents of this theory also espouse that ancient construction projects like the pyramids and Stonehenge are clearly too advanced and a little too fantastic for ancient man to have constructed without help from a more advanced civilization. Or that since there are pyramids in the Americas, Egypt, and China... you guessed it, they all got their idea from aliens. Other popular sites include the Moai of Easter Island and the Nazca Lines. The Unfortunate Implications of this trope are (or at least were) sadly not often highlighted. Not only does it imply multiple people cannot come up with similar ideas independently note (such as various cultures constructing pyramids with no contact with each other. A pyramid is simply a very stable structure a grasp of physics can demonstrate. Egyptian, Cambodian, and Mayan pyramids were all constructed very differently, at different times, and served different purposes) , but very often this trope gets tagged onto "primitive" societies. Saying "aliens did it!" is more or less implying the cultures were too primitive or unsophisticated to come up with devices or structures themselves. This trope being applied to chiefly cultures outside of modern "developed" societies more or less implies those ancient cultures were too backwards to invent anything. This is most consistently a Eurocentric argument. A foolproof way to shut down such an argument is to point to the lack of pyramids in Europe or any impressive built artificial structures that date back before antiquity (we don't really know when Stonehenge was built and it's just a circle of very heavy stones), the only notable exception being those built in ancient Greece which was a melting pot of cultures even then. This would imply that Europeans were late to the game. But, if the "aliens did it" argument is put forth, it would imply that the aliens must not have liked Europeans enough to help them out. Of course, some theorists do say aliens inspired European cultures as well, and it's not uncommon for the Norse or the Greek gods (or other figures from European mythology ) to be portrayed as aliens in fiction as well. Popularized by Erich von Däniken's 1968 book Chariots of the Gods? and its various sequels, which stridently insist that a careful blend of selected archaeological evidence will reveal that this very scenario happened in reality. The Real Life evidence for this is little but if it's brought up in fiction, expect these theories to be always right on the money . After all, it's not like either aliens or ancient humans would be interesting enough to write fiction about on their own, without them interacting, would they? This theory is often crossed over with Atlantis . Overlap with Space Romans is common too, making them aesthetically and culturally similar to the humans they met and influenced. Essentially runs on God Guise . May result in Boldly Coming . When the aliens were the inspiration for our myths and legends, that makes their home planet Where the Magic Went . If the unknown ancestors sport special abilities which aren't derived from technology, compare Our Ancestors Are Superheroes . Usually overlaps with Neglectful Precursors , as the aliens are generally implied to have lost interest in humans and/or concluded we're unworthy successors , ages ago. See also Humanity Came from Space . Sub-Trope of All Theories Are True . See also Beethoven Was an Alien Spy , for individuals rather than entire societies. Compare and contrast Doing In the Wizard , a trope about things being retconned from supernatural to something more scientific In-Universe . Ancient Astronauts are very frequently builders/users of Sufficiently Advanced Bamboo Technology .
